Spotify Track Popularity: Multiple OLS Regression (2020)

Author

Assem Mendygaziyeva

Published

October 15, 2025

Introduction

The year 2020 marked dramatic changes in music consumption, driven by the COVID‑19 pandemic and related lockdowns. With people spending more time at home and live music events disappearing, Spotify became a key platform for discovering, enjoying, and coping with music.

This analysis focuses on tracks released in 2020 within the rock, EDM, hip‑hop, and acoustic genres, and examines how features such as danceability, energy, mode, and genre are associated with track popularity. The goal is to understand which musical characteristics were most strongly linked to popularity during this unique period.

The central hypothesis is that tracks with higher danceability, greater energy, major mode, and specific genres (particularly hip‑hop) are associated with increased popularity.

Load Data / Packages

spotify <- read.csv("spotify_data.csv", header = TRUE)
library(moments)
library(ggplot2)
Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.5.2
library(PerformanceAnalytics)

library(pwr)

Subset to 4 genres AND year 2020

sort(unique(spotify$genre))
 [1] "acoustic"          "afrobeat"          "alt-rock"         
 [4] "ambient"           "black-metal"       "blues"            
 [7] "breakbeat"         "cantopop"          "chicago-house"    
[10] "chill"             "classical"         "club"             
[13] "comedy"            "country"           "dance"            
[16] "dancehall"         "death-metal"       "deep-house"       
[19] "detroit-techno"    "disco"             "drum-and-bass"    
[22] "dub"               "dubstep"           "edm"              
[25] "electro"           "electronic"        "emo"              
[28] "folk"              "forro"             "french"           
[31] "funk"              "garage"            "german"           
[34] "gospel"            "goth"              "grindcore"        
[37] "groove"            "guitar"            "hard-rock"        
[40] "hardcore"          "hardstyle"         "heavy-metal"      
[43] "hip-hop"           "house"             "indian"           
[46] "indie-pop"         "industrial"        "jazz"             
[49] "k-pop"             "metal"             "metalcore"        
[52] "minimal-techno"    "new-age"           "opera"            
[55] "party"             "piano"             "pop"              
[58] "pop-film"          "power-pop"         "progressive-house"
[61] "psych-rock"        "punk"              "punk-rock"        
[64] "rock"              "rock-n-roll"       "romance"          
[67] "sad"               "salsa"             "samba"            
[70] "sertanejo"         "show-tunes"        "singer-songwriter"
[73] "ska"               "sleep"             "songwriter"       
[76] "soul"              "spanish"           "swedish"          
[79] "tango"             "techno"            "trance"           
[82] "trip-hop"         
spotify_sub <- subset(spotify, genre %in% c("rock", "edm", "hip-hop", "acoustic") & year == 2020)

sum(is.na(spotify_sub$popularity))
[1] 0
sum(is.na(spotify_sub$energy))
[1] 0
sum(is.na(spotify_sub$danceability))
[1] 0
sum(is.na(spotify_sub$mode))
[1] 0

boxplot(spotify_sub$popularity, main="Popularity")
boxplot(spotify_sub$danceability, main="Energy")

Regression Analysis

Genre Dummies

#rock reference
spotify_sub$genre_edm      <- ifelse(spotify_sub$genre == "edm", 1, 0)
spotify_sub$genre_hiphop   <- ifelse(spotify_sub$genre == "hip-hop", 1, 0)
spotify_sub$genre_acoustic <- ifelse(spotify_sub$genre == "acoustic", 1, 0)
table(spotify_sub$genre, spotify_sub$genre_edm)
          
             0   1
  acoustic 951   0
  edm        0 446
  hip-hop  854   0
  rock     147   0
table(spotify_sub$genre, spotify_sub$genre_hiphop)
          
             0   1
  acoustic 951   0
  edm      446   0
  hip-hop    0 854
  rock     147   0
table(spotify_sub$genre, spotify_sub$genre_acoustic)
          
             0   1
  acoustic   0 951
  edm      446   0
  hip-hop  854   0
  rock     147   0

regression model

spotify_model <- lm(popularity ~ genre_edm + genre_hiphop + genre_acoustic + energy + danceability + mode, data = spotify_sub)
summary(spotify_model)

Call:
lm(formula = popularity ~ genre_edm + genre_hiphop + genre_acoustic + 
    energy + danceability + mode, data = spotify_sub)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-17.392  -6.149  -1.144   5.044  38.093 

Coefficients:
               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)     46.3517     1.2088  38.346  < 2e-16 ***
genre_edm       -5.1579     0.8019  -6.432 1.52e-10 ***
genre_hiphop     5.6733     0.7796   7.277 4.60e-13 ***
genre_acoustic -19.9936     0.7993 -25.013  < 2e-16 ***
energy          -0.1682     0.9589  -0.175    0.861    
danceability     6.6001     1.2870   5.128 3.16e-07 ***
mode            -0.4515     0.3669  -1.230    0.219    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 8.262 on 2391 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6762,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6754 
F-statistic: 832.1 on 6 and 2391 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Power test

pwr.f2.test(u =4,
v = 2397,
f2 = 2.08 ,
sig.level = 0.01,
power = NULL
)

     Multiple regression power calculation 

              u = 4
              v = 2397
             f2 = 2.08
      sig.level = 0.01
          power = 1

Regression Plots

chart.Correlation(spotify_sub[, c("popularity", "energy", "danceability")], histogram=TRUE, pch=19)
plot(spotify_model)
ggplot(spotify_sub, aes(x = danceability, y = popularity, color = genre)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Figure 1: Popularity vs Danceability by Genre", x = "Danceability", y = "Popularity")
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
spotify_sub$mode <- as.factor(spotify_sub$mode)
ggplot(spotify_sub, aes(x = energy, y = popularity, color = mode)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(aes(group = mode, color = mode), method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Figure 2: Popularity vs Energy by Mode", x = "Energy", y = "Popularity")
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

The echo: false option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).
